result:
Most of what we experience is determined by things we don’t consciously choose.

You didn’t choose your parents, your early environment, the cultural stories that soaked into you before you could argue with them, your native language, or the initial wiring of your temperament. You didn’t choose which events burned themselves into your memory so deeply that they feel like “who you are,” nor which ones quietly dissolved into the background.

Yet, sitting here now, you do have some choice. Not an infinite, fantasy choice that ignores biology, history, or circumstance—but a narrow, real band of influence. It’s that thin strip of freedom that becomes interesting, because it’s where almost everything that feels like “your life” actually happens.

That thin strip looks like:

- What you pay attention to.
- How you interpret what you notice.
- What small action you take next.

Attention is upstream of almost everything else. What we repeatedly attend to becomes familiar, and what is familiar becomes “true,” and what is “true” starts to feel inevitable.

If you routinely attend to what is broken, unfair, or threatening, the world will appear mostly broken, unfair, and threatening. This isn’t positive-thinking advice; it’s just how nervous systems work. The brain is a pattern amplifier.

The catch is that attention is lazy. It defaults to the loudest thing: the notification, the irritation, the self-criticism, the unfinished task. If you never deliberately steer it, your experience becomes a reflection of whatever shouts the loudest into your mind.

So the practical question is not “What is the meaning of life?” but something simpler and more usable:

“What do I want my attention to be hired to do today?”

If you don’t decide, someone else will. An algorithm will. A habit will. An anxiety will.

This doesn’t mean forcing yourself into a relentless grind or a bland optimism. It means noticing that each time you return your attention—back to the work you care about, the person in front of you, the body you’re inhabiting, the value you claim to hold—you are exercising almost all of the freedom you actually have.

Interpretation is the second layer. Reality offers events; the mind supplies explanations. You get cut off in traffic. That’s the event. The explanation might be:

- “People are garbage.”
- “Everyone’s stressed; maybe they didn’t see me.”
- “I should leave earlier so I’m not this tense.”

The event is the same; the inner world that grows from it is not. Some explanations shrink your life, some open it. Most of the time, you can’t immediately access “the truth” about why something happened, but you can usually pick a story that’s at least not self-poisoning.

Then there’s small action. Huge transformations are mostly mythology stitched together from hundreds of inconspicuous moves. You don’t experience “becoming patient”; you experience “this time I paused before I replied.” You don’t “get in shape”; you “went for a 10‑minute walk even though you were tired.” You don’t “find your purpose”; you “spent 20 minutes today doing something that feels non-trivial to you.”

The interesting thing about small actions is that they don’t just change your circumstances; they change your sense of who you are. A person who writes one paragraph every day eventually stops saying “I can’t stick to things” with the same conviction. Identity lags behind behavior, but it follows it.

So a large, abstract question like “How should I live?” can, without losing its seriousness, be reframed as:

- What am I repeatedly feeding my attention?
- What kinds of stories do I habitually tell myself about what happens?
- What is one small, non-heroic move I’m willing to make today in the direction I claim to care about?

Life will still be mostly determined by things you didn’t choose. But in that narrow band where you do have some say, there’s quite a lot of room.

If you want, tell me a corner of your life you’re currently puzzled by—work, relationships, creativity, motivation—and I can help you explore how those three levers (attention, interpretation, small action) might apply there.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
